coredata.dc:title: High-Performance green fluorescent carbon Dots: Synthesis, Properties, and applications in temperature Sensing, and information anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yingying
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) possess advantages such as high fluorescence quantum yield and non-toxicity, making them one of the most promising luminescent nanomaterials for anti-counterfeiting applications. In this research, a high-performance green fluorescent carbon dot (G-CDs) was successfully synthesized via a solvothermal method using m-Phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors. The synthesized G-CDs exhibit remarkable optical properties, with a maximum excitation wavelength of 439 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 507 nm, accompanied by an impressive quantum yield of 30.92 %. G-CDs demonstrate exceptional stability, retaining their fluorescence characteristics under various environmental conditions, including light exposure, temperature fluctuations, different pH values, and salt concentrations. We further explored the potential applications of G-CDs in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks, and temperature sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvothermal method
Quantum yield
Multipurpose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of G-CDs. (a) TEM image, with high-resolution TEM. (b) Particle size distribution. (c) XRD pattern of G-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum. (e) XPS survey spectrum. (f-h) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and P2p, respectively.

Optical characteristics of G-CDs. (a) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum and (b) two-dimensional projection. (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (blue), and emission spectrum (multi-colored). (d) Normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.

Stability measurements of G-CDs. Effect of (a) UV light irradiation, (b) time, (c) NaCl concentration, and (d) pH on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs. (e) Selectivity of G-CDs towards various interfering substances.

Influence of solvents on luminescence performance. (a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) Maximum emission wavelengths of G-CDs in different solvents. (c) Plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance. (d) Comparison of quantum yields in different solvents.

(a) Fluorescent carbon dot solution used as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink for inkjet printers, stamps, and pens. Content written with G-CDs as fluorescent ink: (b) invisible under daylight, (c) visible under ultraviolet light.
Temperature sensing application. (a) Fluorescence spectra at different temperatures. (b) Change in fluorescence intensity with temperature. (c) Stern-Volmer fitting standard curve.

head.ce:title: High-Performance green fluorescent carbon Dots: Synthesis, Properties, and applications in temperature Sensing, and information anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess advantages such as high fluorescence quantum yield and non-toxicity, making them one of the most promising luminescent nanomaterials for anti-counterfeiting applications. In this research, a high-performance green fluorescent carbon dot (G-CDs) was successfully synthesized via a solvothermal method using m-Phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors. The synthesized G-CDs exhibit remarkable optical properties, with a maximum excitation wavelength of 439 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 507 nm, accompanied by an impressive quantum yield of 30.92 %. G-CDs demonstrate exceptional stability, retaining their fluorescence characteristics under various environmental conditions, including light exposure, temperature fluctuations, different pH values, and salt concentrations. We further explored the potential applications of G-CDs in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks, and temperature sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of nanoscale carbon-based fluorescent material, typically with a particle size less than 10 nm[1,2]. They consist of a carbon core and abundant surface defects and functional groups. Since their accidental discovery in 2004, CDs have attracted widespread attention from researchers due to their unique optical properties, good biocompatibility, excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, and simple preparation methods[3,4], becoming a research hotspot in nano fluorescent materials. CDs exhibit fluorescence properties similar to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, but without the need for heavy metal elements, making them cost-effective and environmentally friendly[5]. The luminescence mechanism of CDs mainly originates from quantum confinement effects, defect emission, and intramolecular conversion. By controlling the size, morphology, doping elements, and surface groups of CDs, a wide range of fluorescence emission from ultraviolet to near-infrared can be achieved, and their optical properties, such as fluorescence intensity and lifetime, can be adjusted[6]. In recent years, CDs have shown broad application prospects in many fields, including biomedical imaging[7,8], optoelectronic devices[9–11], energy conversion[12–14], chemical/biosensing[15,16], and photocatalysis[17–19]. Particularly in biomedical imaging, the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of CDs make them potential new fluorescent probes[20,21]. Overall, as a new type of nano carbonaceous material, CDs possess unique optical properties and excellent biocompatibility, making them a research hotspot and frontier in interdisciplinary studies.

The optical properties of CDs are closely related to the type of precursors, reaction media, and synthesis conditions[22–24]. By doping CDs with elements, the surface functional groups and electronic structure can be effectively adjusted, thereby improving the fluorescence quantum yield and optimizing their luminescence performance[25]. Common non-metallic doping elements for CDs include nitrogen[26], sulfur[27], boron[28], phosphorus[29], and others[30,31]. Among them, nitrogen is one of the most common doping elements. Due to the similar atomic radius of N and C, high electronegativity, and unique electron configuration, introducing N atoms into the CD structure can increase the electron density, induce changes in the internal electron environment, and achieve the purpose of improving the luminescence performance of CDs and promoting a red-shift in the emission wavelength. Meanwhile, phosphorus doping can introduce new luminescence centers and defect states, increase the capture-transfer-recombination processes within CDs, and further enhance their quantum yield and photostability[32].

In this work, we report the synthesis of high-performance green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorus using m-Phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors via a solvothermal method. The quantum yield of G-CDs reaches up to 30.92 %. We extensively investigate the morphology, structure, optical properties, and luminescence mechanisms of G-CDs. Moreover, we explore the potential applications of G-CDs in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks, temperature sensing. Experimental results show that G-CDs exhibit bright green fluorescence under ultraviolet light, providing a new fluorescent ink material for information security and anti-counterfeiting applications. Due to the linear response of the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs to temperature changes, they can be used as highly sensitive temperature sensors for monitoring temperature variations in special environments such as chemical and aerospace industries. This work lays the foundation for the preparation of high-performance fluorescent carbon dots and expands their applications in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, and temperature sensing.

M−Phenylenediamine and quinine sulfate (analytical grade, ≥99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Phosphoric acid, ethanol (EtOH, analytical grade, ≥99.5 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, analytical grade, ≥99.5 %), tetrahydrofuran (THF, analytical grade, ≥99.5 %), and acetone (AC, analytical grade,≥99.5 %) were obtained from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All water used in the experiments was double-distilled water prepared in-house.

A 25 mL hydrothermal reaction vessel (stainless − steel outer shell with a polytetrafluoroethylene lining, capable of withstanding high temperatures and pressures up to 300 °C and 50 MPa) was procured from Xinniu Technology Co., Ltd. An ultrasonic cleaning machine (with a frequency of 40 kHz and a power of 300 W) was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. An electric constant temperature blast drying oven (temperature range: room temperature —300 °C, temperature control accuracy: ±1 °C) was acquired from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. A UV–visible spectrophotometer (wavelength range: 325–1000 nm, wavelength accuracy: ±2 nm) was obtained from Shanghai Shunyu Hengping Scientific Instruments. The Lumina F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer (excitation wavelength range: 190–650 nm, emission wavelength range: 200–800 nm, wavelength accuracy: ±1 nm) was purchased from Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd.

In this study, a solvothermal method was employed to successfully synthesize green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) using m-Phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors with ethanol as the solvent. The specific synthesis steps are as follows: 0.22 g of m-Phenylenediamine (2 mmol) and 1.046 mL of phosphoric acid (2 mmol) were weighed in a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined container. 10 mL of ethanol was added as the solvent. After ultrasonication for 10 min, the lined container was transferred to a stainless steel high-temperature reaction vessel and kept in a 180℃ air oven for 6 h. After natural cooling, the product was purified by column chromatography to obtain green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs). Fig. 1 illustrates the entire synthesis process flow, clearly depicting each step from precursor preparation to final product purification.

A suite of analytical techniques including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to characterize the structure of G-CDs, revealing their unique crystalline structure, surface functional groups, elemental composition, and chemical states. This laid a solid foundation for further investigating their properties and applications. Fig. 2 a displays the TEM image of G-CDs, where the high-resolution TEM image in the top left corner shows clear lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon. The particle size distribution plot in Fig. 2b indicates that the average particle size of G-CDs is approximately 6.5 nm. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of G-CDs, shown in Fig. 2c, exhibits a prominent diffraction peak at around 25°, which can be assigned to the (002) plane of graphene, suggesting that G-CDs possess a graphene-like layered structure. Additionally, the absence of other significant diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern indicates that G-CDs contain an amorphous structure in addition to the graphene-like crystalline structure, which is a typical characteristic of carbon dots. The FT-IR spectrum provides information about the surface functional groups of G-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, G-CDs exhibit a broad absorption band in the range of 3300–3562 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and C-H. The absorption peaks at 1641 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibrations of C=O and CH3/CH2, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1022 cm−1 and 906 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of C-P and C-O-C, indicating the successful doping of phosphorus into the carbon dots. To further investigate the surface composition of the G-CDs sample, XPS spectra of G-CDs were acquired. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows characteristic peaks of O1s, C1s, and P2p at binding energies of 531.7 eV, 286.6 eV, and 133.1 eV, respectively, confirming that G-CDs are primarily composed of C, O, and P elements. Quantitative analysis of the atomic ratios reveals that the atomic percentages of C, O, and P are 22.33 %, 60.80 %, and 16.88 %, respectively, further confirming the successful doping of phosphorus. By deconvoluting the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and P2p, more detailed information about the surface chemical states of G-CDs can be obtained. The C1s peak (Fig. 2f) is deconvoluted into three components: 284.6 eV (C-C/C=C), 286.08 eV (C-O-C), and 288.9 eV (O-C=O). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 533.32 eV (O=C) and 531.89 eV (O-C). The P2p spectrum (Fig. 2h) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 135.19 eV (P-C) and 134.31 eV (P-O). These results unveil the chemical environment on the surface of G-CDs, providing valuable information for understanding their properties and potential applications.

The optical properties of G-CDs were analyzed in detail using three-dimensional fluorescence spectra with two-dimensional projections, UV–vis absorption spectra, excitation spectra, and emission spectra. Fig. 3 a and 3b show the three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum and two-dimensional projection of G-CDs, respectively. It can be clearly observed that G-CDs exhibit only one excitation and emission peak, with the excitation region mainly located between 395–495 nm and the emission region primarily between 450–600 nm. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum, and emission spectrum of G-CDs are presented in Fig. 3c. From the UV–vis absorption spectrum, G-CDs exhibit strong absorption bands below 400 nm, which are typical aromatic benzene ring absorption peaks originating from π-π* electronic transitions of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. A broad absorption band can also be observed in the 400–470 nm region. The maximum peak of this broad absorption band is located at 437 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* transitions of surface states containing C=O, C-O/C-P structures. The excitation and emission spectra reveal that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs are 439 nm and 507 nm, respectively. Therefore, for highly sensitive detection, an excitation wavelength of 439 nm was set as the optimal value throughout the experiments. To investigate the luminescence mechanism of G-CDs, the emission spectra were measured by varying the excitation wavelength in the range of 400–500 nm and normalized, as shown in Fig. 3d. It can be observed that the emission wavelengths of G-CDs nearly overlap under different excitation wavelengths, exhibiting an excitation-independent emission behavior. This indicates that G-CDs possess highly stable emission properties, almost independent of the excitation wavelength. This “wavelength stability” of luminescence may be related to the surface chemical structure and morphology of the carbon dots, as uniform surface states contribute to maintaining stable fluorescence emission characteristics.

To comprehensively understand the stability characteristics of G-CDs, we conducted a series of experiments under conditions of light irradiation, low temperature, different salt concentrations, and varying pH values, to thoroughly investigate the stability of G-CDs. Firstly, the anti-photobleaching property was analyzed. Under continuous ultraviolet light irradiation (365 nm), we observed the change in fluorescence intensity of the G-CDs solution over 60 min. As shown in Fig. 4 a, even under continuous UV light exposure, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs did not exhibit significant decay, indicating their excellent photostability. Furthermore, the stability of G-CDs at low temperatures was investigated. The G-CDs solution was placed in a 4 ℃ refrigerator, and its fluorescence intensity was measured at different time points. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, although the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly over time, the change was negligible, suggesting that G-CDs remain stable in low-temperature environments. Subsequently, the salt resistance of G-CDs was studied by testing their fluorescence intensity in NaCl solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 1000 mM. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs remained stable over a wide range of salt concentrations without significant decay, confirming their ability to maintain good luminescence performance even in high sodium ion environments. The stability of G-CDs under different pH conditions was also evaluated. As presented in Fig. 4d, within the pH range of 1 to 10, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs showed minimal variation, indicating their good stability in this pH range. However, when the pH increased to 11, the fluorescence intensity slightly decreased, and at pH 12, the intensity decreased by approximately one-third. This suggests that while G-CDs can exist stably within the pH range of 1 to 10, special considerations may be required under highly alkaline conditions. In practical applications, G-CDs may encounter various interfering substances, such as metal ions and antibiotics. We conducted experiments to assess the selectivity of G-CDs towards these common interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 4e, various metal ions had minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs, indicating their good anti-interference ability. In summary, G-CDs exhibited excellent stability and anti-interference capability under different environmental conditions, including light exposure, temperature variations, salt concentrations, pH changes, and in the presence of interfering substances. These properties make G-CDs a highly promising material for widespread applications in biomedical and environmental monitoring fields.

Solvent is a key factor in determining the luminescence properties of carbon dots (G-CDs). In this study, five common solvents were selected: water (Water), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH), acetone (AC), and tetrahydrofuran (THF). These solvents possess distinct physical and chemical properties, providing diverse environmental conditions for G-CDs. Fig. 5 a displays the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs in different solvents. It can be observed that G-CDs exhibit the highest fluorescence intensity in water and the lowest fluorescence intensity in DMF. Additionally, the maximum emission wavelengths of G-CDs in these solvents were statistically analyzed, as shown in Fig. 5b, revealing that the emission wavelengths are relatively close, ranging from 501 nm to 510 nm. To accurately evaluate the impact of different solvents on the luminescence performance of G-CDs, quantum yield measurements were conducted. By measuring the fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra of G-CDs in each solvent, and performing linear fitting of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance (Fig. 5c), the quantum yields of G-CDs in different solvents were calculated (Fig. 5d). The results demonstrate significant differences in the quantum yields of G-CDs among the solvents. In water, the quantum yield of G-CDs reached the highest value of 30.92 %, while in DMF, the quantum yield was the lowest at 3.56 %. Furthermore, the quantum yield in ethanol was relatively high at 28.85 %, while those in acetone and tetrahydrofuran were 9.75 % and 5.97 %, respectively. From the experimental results, it is evident that the quantum yield of G-CDs is closely related to the nature of the solvent. Water and ethanol, as protic solvents, provide a favorable microenvironment that enhances the luminescence of G-CDs. In contrast, DMF, AC, and THF, as non-protic solvents, may lead to a decrease in the luminescence performance of G-CDs due to differences in the microscopic structure and intermolecular interactions. Selecting an appropriate solvent is crucial for improving the luminescence efficiency of G-CDs. These findings provide important reference information for the applications of G-CDs in areas such as bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, and sensors.

Fluorescent carbon dots have widespread applications in various fields, such as display devices, environmental detection, and chemical sensing. One of the most common applications is their direct use as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks. Fig. 6 demonstrates the potential and application of fluorescent carbon dot solutions as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks. As a UV-encrypted fluorescent ink, G-CDs can be directly used in inkjet printers, stamps, or pens (Fig. 6a). The content written with G-CDs is nearly invisible under daylight, showing only faint traces, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. However, upon exposure to ultraviolet light (20w), these previously invisible markings become clearly visible, as shown in Fig. 6c. Under the influence of UV light, the G-CDs fluorescent ink emits bright fluorescence, revealing the hidden information. This visible/invisible transition under different lighting conditions provides an effective means for anti-counterfeiting technology. By utilizing G-CDs, a fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial as an anti-counterfeiting ink, effective prevention of copying and counterfeiting can be achieved, enhancing security for product traceability, copyright protection, and more. Additionally, the advantages of easy preparation and environmental friendliness further promote the application prospects of G-CDs in the anti-counterfeiting field.

Fluorescent carbon dots have a broad application prospect in the field of sensing, enabling the detection and monitoring of various biological/chemical molecules, such as nucleic acids, proteins, and various small molecules. They can also be used for detecting heavy metal ions (e.g., mercury, lead, cadmium) and organic pollutants in water environments, providing a convenient and highly sensitive new fluorescent probe for environmental monitoring. In this experiment, we explored a novel application of G-CDs in temperature sensing based on their linear response to temperature changes. By measuring the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs in the temperature range of 20 °C to 60 °C, their temperature sensing performance can be evaluated and optimized. Through measuring the fluorescence spectra at different temperatures (Fig. 7 a) and recording the changes in fluorescence intensity, the experimental results showed that the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs gradually decreased with increasing temperature within the range of 20 °C to 60 °C, as depicted in Fig. 7b. This relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation, with the fitting equation: (I0-I)/I0 = 0.0043 T − 0.0821. Linear regression analysis of the experimental data revealed an excellent linear relationship between the decrease in fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and the increase in temperature, with a correlation coefficient as high as 0.9919. This result indicates that G-CDs can serve as a highly accurate temperature sensor for precisely monitoring and recording temperature changes in special scenarios, such as biomedical applications, environmental monitoring, and industrial production. Furthermore, owing to the low production cost of G-CDs and their good biocompatibility and environmental friendliness, they offer a high cost-performance ratio in practical applications.

In this work, green fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized via a solvothermal method using m-Phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors. The prepared carbon dots possess a surface rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. In ethanol, the maximum excitation wavelength is 439 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength is 507 nm, exhibiting excitation wavelength dependence. Notably, the G-CDs demonstrate excellent stability, maintaining their properties under UV light exposure (365 nm), high NaCl concentrations, prolonged storage times, pH range of 1 to 10, and various interfering substances. The fluorescence intensity of these carbon dots varies in different solvents, exhibiting a higher quantum yield in protic solvents, with a maximum of 30.92 %, while showing lower quantum yields in non-protic solvents. Within the temperature range of 20 °C to 60 °C, the synthesized G-CDs exhibit a remarkable linear relationship, with a linear regression correlation coefficient of 0.9919. Furthermore, these carbon dots can be utilized as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks.
Yingying Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.
